reasoning: Although the U.S. Supreme Court has ordered the U.S. government to facilitate Kilmar Abrego Garcia's return, he is currently held in an El Salvador maximum security prison (CECOT). Both the Trump administration (via AG Pam Bondi) and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele have publicly and strongly refused to facilitate his return, arguing that he is under El Salvador's jurisdiction and will not be released. While the presiding federal judge (Paula Xinis) lifted a stay on discovery as of April 30 and ordered the government to produce sworn testimony in May regarding its efforts, the executive branch plans to resist these orders by invoking foreign policy privileges (as indicated by Secretary of State Marco Rubio). Since the U.S. judiciary cannot physically compel a foreign sovereign nation to release a prisoner, and given the protracted timeline typical of high-level constitutional and diplomatic disputes, it is highly improbable that this standoff will be resolved with Garcia returning to U.S. soil in the mere 60 days remaining before the July 1, 2025, deadline. The chance of resolution relies heavily on an unexpected diplomatic reversal, which seems very unlikely given the current political posturing of both administrations involved.
